judgments.reasoning: No candidate addresses try-catch around dynamic import failures; all candidates focus on the forEach-async pattern, not import error handling.

Candidate 1 identifies the identical forEach-async fire-and-forget issue with the same mechanism, consequence, and remediation (replace with for...of or Promise.all), and notes the pattern appears across the PR.
Candidate 4 identifies the exact same issue: the default export in BackupCode.tsx is named TwoFactor, causing naming collision and ambiguity in DevTools.
No candidate notes that the error message references 'backup code login' instead of 'disable' context.
Candidate 3 identifies the same case-sensitive indexOf comparison and notes that lowercase hex codes reject valid uppercase user input, with the same fix of adding .toLowerCase().
Candidate 2 identifies the same TOCTOU race condition on the backup-code read-modify-write path allowing concurrent requests to both consume the same code.
Candidate 3 identifies the exact crash: destructuring mainHostDestinationCalendar from a potentially empty/null array yields undefined, then .integration access throws TypeError.
No candidate identifies that mainHostDestinationCalendar?.integration uses redundant optional chaining given the prior ternary already guarantees the value is truthy.
Candidate 1 identifies the same dead conditional: the else-branch compares cal.externalId against the same falsy externalCalendarId value, so .find() never matches.
No candidate addresses the IS_TEAM_BILLING_ENABLED logic inversion causing slug/requestedSlug to be set under the wrong condition.
Candidate 6 identifies the same interface incompatibility: LarkCalendarService.createEvent still uses the old single-argument signature while the Calendar interface now requires credentialId.
No candidates exist for this PR.
No candidates exist for this PR.
Candidate 1 identifies the same issue: Ruby silently discards the first self.downsize definition when a second definition with the same name is declared, breaking all callers of the 4-arg signature.
Candidate 4 identifies the same problem: maxSizeKB is hardcoded to 10*1024 instead of reading from Discourse.SiteSettings per upload type, causing divergence from admin-configured limits.
No candidate addresses the specific failure mode where passing 80% as dimensions breaks animated GIF downsizing via gifsicle --resize-fit.
Candidate 1 identifies the same nil pointer: TopicUser.find_by returns nil when no row exists, and the immediately following tu.notification_level call crashes with NoMethodError.
No candidate identifies the typo 'stopNotificiationsText' (missing 'n' in Notifications).

Candidate 2 identifies the same issue: should_block? performs a DB write (incrementing match_count) as a side effect of what should be a pure read predicate, making the method name misleading and causing inflated counts.
No candidate identifies the regex suffix-matching vulnerability where evil.example.com matches a whitelist entry for example.com.
Candidate 5 identifies the same SSRF/local-file-read vulnerability: Kernel#open(url) in import_remote accepts any URI scheme including file://, with no scheme validation before the open call.
No candidate identifies the indexOf-based origin validation bypass where a domain like evil-discourseUrl.com passes the check.
No candidate addresses the postMessage targetOrigin being the full referrer URL rather than scheme+host+port.
No candidate identifies the X-Frame-Options: ALLOWALL clickjacking vulnerability or the referer-based fallback masking validation failures.

Candidate 6 identifies the stored XSS via unescaped URL interpolation in raw_html cook method, which corresponds to the XSS part of golden 5 (the nil contents issue is not covered, but XSS via unescaped url is the same core finding).
Candidate 2 identifies the exact same issue: 'end if' in the ERB template is invalid Ruby syntax that causes a SyntaxError crash on every render of that template.
Candidate 1 identifies the same TOCTOU race: CountDevices is read outside a transaction, allowing concurrent requests to both observe count < limit and both INSERT, exceeding the limit.

Candidate 2 identifies that TagDevice is now synchronous and uses caller context, so client disconnects or limit-reached errors now block/fail authentication rather than being non-blocking background work.
No candidate identifies the compile error where dbSession.Exec receives a []interface{} splat instead of a string first parameter.
No candidate identifies that returning ErrDeviceLimitReached when no rows were updated is misleading because the device may not exist.
No candidate addresses the time window calculation inconsistency between device.UpdatedAt.UTC().Add(-expiration) and time.Now().UTC().
The sole candidate addresses metric recording drift, not the asymmetric cache trust logic where cached grants bypass database lookup but cached denials do not.
No candidate identifies the test comment incorrectly stating cached permissions 'allow access' when the map stores false for the tested scope.
No candidate addresses ContextualLoggerMiddleware panicking on nil request; candidates cover traceID logging and TestLogger issues.
Candidate 1 identifies the same issue: traceID extraction was removed from the old logger and not added to the new instrumentContext, so traceID is silently absent from all plugin request logs.

Candidate 2 identifies the same test weakness: applyTemplateVariables is asserted only by call count without verifying the interpolated result actually propagates to runQuery, which corresponds to the golden's concern that the filters parameter is untested.
Candidate 1 identifies the same compile error: isConditionalPasskeysEnabled() is called with no arguments but only a one-argument overload (requiring UserModel) is defined.

Candidate 2 identifies the same behavioral regression: the new isConditionalPasskeysEnabled(user) requires user != null, so fillContextForm is no longer called on initial page load where no user is identified, breaking anonymous passkey discovery.
No candidate identifies the recursive caching call using session instead of delegate, which would be a stack overflow or incorrect delegation issue.
Candidate 4 identifies the exact same bug: the cleanup loop registers literal string 'alias' instead of interpolating the loop variable to produce 'idp-alias-'+i, so no IDPs are cleaned up.
Candidate 3 identifies the same defect: getBouncyCastleProvider returns a SUN/SunJSSE provider from KeyStore.getDefaultType() instead of an actual BouncyCastle provider.
Candidate 1 identifies the same dead code: two ASN1Encoder.create().write() calls whose return values are immediately discarded before the real encoding in the return statement.
No candidate identifies the picocli.exit() / System.exit() direct call problem; candidates cover exit code renumbering, bare string literals, and incomplete test coverage.
Candidate 1 identifies the same Django ORM negative-slicing crash: the negative-offset branch passes a negative start_offset to queryset slicing, which raises ValueError.
Candidate 4 identifies the same null-dereference: organization_context.member.has_global_access is accessed unconditionally when member can be None for token-authenticated users.
Candidate 2 identifies the same TypeError: get_item_key passes a datetime value to math.floor/math.ceil, which datetime objects do not support.
Candidate 1 identifies the same OptimizedCursorPaginator negative-offset Django ORM slicing crash with the same mechanism and evidence.
No candidate specifically identifies BasePaginator's negative-offset branch as a separate finding; candidate 1 addresses OptimizedCursorPaginator only.
No candidate in this PR addresses get_item_key floor/ceil TypeError on datetime; candidates 2 and 3 cover unrelated issues (KeyError and redirect depth).
No candidate identifies that sample_rate = 0.0 is falsy and skipped by a truthiness guard; candidates address implementation location and bare-except handling.
No candidate identifies the non-deterministic Python hash() used for cache key construction causing cross-process invalidation failures.
No candidate identifies the upsampling eligibility check using the outer dataset instead of the scoped_dataset.
The sole candidate covers safe_urlopen exception handling, not a null reference from missing github_authenticated_user state.
No candidate identifies the OAuth state using pipeline.signature (static) instead of a per-request random value.
No candidate identifies the KeyError from accessing integration.metadata[sender][login] without checking for sender key existence.
